content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES OF

PILLSBURY, MADISON & SUTRO
POST OFFICE BOX 7880

OTHER OFFICES
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA
SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA
SAN JOSE, CALIFORNIA
WALNUT CREEK, CALIFORNIA
WASHINGTON, D.C.

SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94120

TELEPHONE (415) 983-1000

CABLE ADDRESS "EVANS"
TELEX 34743
TELECOPIER (415) 398-2096

WRITER'S OFFICE AND
DIRECT DIAL NUMBER

235 Montgomery Street
(415) 983-1585

August 24, 1990

Ms. Carla Wardlow
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Carla:

Pursuant to our conversation on Thursday,
August 16, I would appreciate it if you would confirm your
oral advice with respect to the proper reporting and dis-
closure of attempts to influence "administrative action" in
proceedings before the California Public Utilities Commission (CPUC) under the lobbying disclosure provisions of the
Political Reform Act of 1974 (the "Act").

As a practical matter, persons who become involved
in CPUC proceedings must classify each proceeding at the
outset in order to determine their obligations, if any,
under the Act. The three possible classifications for such
proceedings are: (1) ratemaking; (2) quasi-legislative; or
(3) quasi-judicial.

We understand that, where hearings before the CPUC
in connection with ratemaking proceedings are concerned,
expenses incurred in connection with "administrative testimony" (as defined in 2 Cal.Admin.Code §18239(d)(1)) are
subject to the reduced reporting requirements provided in
2 Cal.Admin.Code §18616(g)(4) (hereinafter the
"§ 18616(g)(4) rule"). In addition, individuals who make
appearances in such proceedings qualify for the limited
exemption from the Act's lobbyist registration requirements
contained in §18239(a) (hereinafter the "§ 18239 exception").

It is also our understanding that, pursuant to the
Selby and Gardner advice letters (A-88-387 and A-90-307,
respectively), participation in workshops and conferences
held in connection with ratemaking proceedings will

10434348

Ms. Carla Wardlow
August 24, 1990
Page 2

generally not qualify as "administrative testimony" (and
hence will not qualify for either the § 18616(g)(4) rule or
the 18239 exception) if there is no notice of the meeting
and no record of the meeting is created. Therefore, full
reporting is required subject to established exceptions,
such as the 10% rule provided in 2 Cal.Admin. Code
§ 18616(f)(1).

As for non-ratemaking quasi-legislative proceed-
ings before the CPUC, it is our understanding that the
underlying proceeding would not be subject to the
§ 18616(g)(4) rule but could qualify for the § 18239 excep-
tion. Ancillary proceedings such as workshops and confer-
ences would be subject to neither the § 18616(g)(4) rule nor
the § 18239 exception. Expenses associated with all such
proceedings would be treated in the same manner as those
associated with all other quasi-legislative proceedings in
California; namely, full reporting is required subject to
established exceptions, such as the 10% rule provided in
2 Cal.Admin.Code §18616(f)(1).

Finally, where non-ratemaking quasi-judicial
proceedings before the CPUC are concerned, no reporting is
required with respect to any expenses associated with those
proceedings or with any conferences or workshops ancillary
thereto, since all such proceedings are outside the scope of
the lobbying disclosure provisions of the Act.

If the foregoing accurately summarizes your
advice, I would appreciate your confirming it at your
earliest convenience.

As always, we appreciate your assistance.

Very truly yours,

Frederick K. Lowell
Frederick K. Lowell

10436348

If the foregoing accurately summarizes your advice, I would appreciate your confirming it at your earliest convenience. As always, we appreciate your assistance. Very truly yours, Frederick K. Lowell
